tree_topic: Shopping > Entertainment Media > DVD & Video Shopping
questions: Trace the legal evolution of the 'first sale' doctrine that underpins the video rental economy, starting with the seminal 1984 Supreme Court decision regarding a specific home recording device. Identify the device and the specific industry leader who famously compared it to the 'Boston Strangler.' Next, connect this to the 2013 Supreme Court ruling involving a Thai student's resale of textbooks; provide the student's name and the specific vote count of that decision. Finally, identify the Massachusetts-based digital resale startup mentioned in the context of these precedents that was sued by Capitol Records, and describe the judge's specific ruling regarding the request for an injunction against the site.

logic_chain: In a Copyright Ruling, the Legacy of the Betamax
rounds.question: Trace the legal evolution of the 'first sale' doctrine that underpins the video rental economy, starting with the seminal 1984 Supreme Court decision regarding a specific home recording device. Identify the device and the specific industry leader who famously compared it to the 'Boston Strangler.' Next, connect this to the 2013 Supreme Court ruling involving a Thai student's resale of textbooks; provide the student's name and the specific vote count of that decision. Finally, identify the Massachusetts-based digital resale startup mentioned in the context of these precedents that was sued by Capitol Records, and describe the judge's specific ruling regarding the request for an injunction against the site.
checklist_width: Comparison Quote: Referenced the 'Boston Strangler' (comparing the VCR to it)
Injunction Ruling: The judge refused to grant the injunction (that would have closed the site)
checklist_depth: Identified the 1984 case entity: Sony Betamax / Sony Corp. v. Universal City Studios
Identified the 2013 case entity: Supap Kirtsaeng / Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons
rounds.rationale: The question requires Deep reasoning to link the 'video rental' domain to its legal origin (the 1984 Betamax case) and then trace that legal logic to a specific 2013 case (Kirtsaeng) and a modern digital example (ReDigi). It is Wide because the answer requires aggregating disparate details (quotes, names, vote counts, and specific court orders) that are scattered throughout the source text.
source_nodes: In a Copyright Ruling, the Legacy of the Betamax - The New York Times
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Business | Copyright Ruling Rings With Echo of Betamax https://www.nytimes.com/2013/03/27/business/in-a-copyright-ruling-the-lingering-legacy-of-the-betamax.html Advertisement
rounds.answer_a:
The 1984 Supreme Court’s Sony Corp. of America v. Universal City Studios, Inc. anchored the first sale doctrine’s role in the video rental economy [1]. The case centered on the Sony Betamax home recording device, with MCA/Universal chief Lew Wasserman infamously labeling it the “Boston Strangler” for its perceived threat to film revenues [1]. The ruling upheld consumers’ right to resell or rent legally acquired media, clearing the way for the booming video rental industry by validating first sale’s application to home-recorded content. In 2013, the doctrine expanded via Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, where Thai student Supap Kirtsaeng won a 6-3 decision that extended first sale protection to foreign-manufactured textbooks he resold in the U.S. [2]. This reinforced resale market viability for educational materials. The Massachusetts-based digital resale startup ReDigi was sued by Capitol Records over its digital music resale platform [3]. Judge Richard J. Sullivan denied Capitol’s preliminary injunction request, ruling ReDigi’s model—transferring original digital files and deleting the seller’s copy—did not conclusively violate copyright law, though he acknowledged lingering uncertainties about first sale’s digital-era scope [3]. This chain of rulings shaped cross-media resale markets, balancing copyright holders’ interests with consumer rights to redistribute legally obtained goods.

## References
[1] Sony Corp. of America v. Universal City Studios, Inc., 464 U.S. 417 (1984)
[2] Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc., 568 U.S. 519 (2013)
[3] Capitol Records, Inc. v. ReDigi Inc., 934 F. Supp. 2d 640 (S.D.N.Y. 2013)
rounds.answer_b:
The "first sale" doctrine's modern contours emerged from *Sony Corp. v. Universal City Studios* (1984), which legalized the Betamax VCR [1]. MPAA president Jack Valenti famously likened the device to the "Boston Strangler" [2]. This precedent enabled physical media rentals by affirming consumers' resale rights.

The doctrine extended internationally in *Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc.* (2013), where Supap Kirtsaeng, a Thai student, legally purchased textbooks abroad and resold them in the U.S. [3]. The Supreme Court ruled 6-3 that first sale applies to lawful copies manufactured overseas, protecting his resale rights [4].

This principle faces challenges in digital markets. Massachusetts-based startup ReDigi created a platform for reselling digital music files. Capitol Records sued in 2013, arguing that digital transfers create unauthorized reproductions [5]. U.S. District Judge Richard Sullivan denied Capitol's request for a preliminary injunction, reasoning that ReDigi's technology, which transmitted files without creating additional copies, potentially complied with copyright law [6]. However, he later ruled the service infringed reproduction rights, distinguishing digital from physical transfer [7].

## References
[1] Sony Corp. v. Universal City Studios, 464 U.S. 417 (1984).
[2] Valenti, J. (1982). Testimony before Congress on home recording.
[3] *Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc.*, 568 U.S. 519 (2013).
[4] Greenhouse, L. (2013). "Justices Back Student on Textbook Sales." *The New York Times*.
[5] Capitol Records, LLC v. ReDigi Inc., No. 12-95 (S.D.N.Y. 2013).
[6] Sullivan, R. J. (2013). Order on Preliminary Injunction.
[7] Capitol Records, LLC v. ReDigi Inc., 934 F. Supp. 2d 640 (S.D.N.Y. 2013).
rounds.judge_reasoning: Agent B is the clear winner because it correctly identified the industry leader who made the 'Boston Strangler' comparison (Jack Valenti). Agent A incorrectly attributed this famous quote to Lew Wasserman. Additionally, Agent B used better formatting (paragraph breaks) compared to Agent A's wall of text, making it easier to read.
